extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a laboratory thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box fitted with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan for air circulation, three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors, and a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B acting as the controller. The system is powered via MOSFETs driven through optocouplers on a custom PCB, and communicates with a RabbitMQ message broker running on the Pi. The incubator is used to maintain a target temperature for biological samples, making it a representative cyber‑physical system for digital‑twin research.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers real‑time visualization of incubator status via a time‑series database and dashboard, on‑demand calibration of the thermal models, anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation using a Kalman filter, what‑if simulations through co‑simulation of historical or live data to optimise controller parameters, and a self‑adaptation loop that detects behavioural changes, schedules experiments, recalibrates models, and re‑configures the controller automatically. These services are aimed at operators, researchers and engineers who monitor, optimise and maintain the incubator.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on a RabbitMQ message broker for inter‑component communication, a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator I/O, a bang‑bang controller implemented on the Raspberry Pi, and a Kalman filter for anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation. Simulation of the thermal dynamics is performed using two models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters), with Model B selected for its superior inertia prediction. Real‑time data are stored in a time‑series database and visualised on a dashboard. The entire stack, including hardware schematics, firmware, and source code, is released as open source on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The incubator’s Digital Twin operates on a second‑scale time horizon. The low‑level driver periodically polls the DS18S20 temperature sensors and publishes readings to RabbitMQ; the controller consumes these messages, applies a bang‑bang control logic, and publishes actuator commands. The fan’s airflow (0.02 m³/s) and the box volume (0.03 m³) imply an air‑mixing time of ≈2 s, which bounds the fastest observable temperature change. No explicit sampling frequency is specified, but the system is designed to react within a few seconds. RabbitMQ’s lightweight AMQP protocol provides sub‑second latency for message delivery, enabling near real‑time synchronization between the physical plant, the controller, the low‑level driver, and the Digital Twin. The Digital Twin updates its state, runs a Kalman filter, performs calibration, and executes what‑if simulations on a timescale of seconds to minutes, depending on the computational load.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture describes a single digital twin instance that subscribes to the RabbitMQ broker on the Raspberry Pi. The controller, low‑level driver, and digital twin are co‑located on the same physical host; no mention of multiple twin instances, decentralized or federated deployments, or hierarchical coordination between twins is made in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows an event‑driven, message‑broker pattern. The Raspberry Pi hosts the controller, low‑level driver, and RabbitMQ server. Sensor readings and actuator commands are exchanged as AMQP messages. The digital twin layer consumes these messages, stores them in a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB or similar, though not named), runs a Kalman filter for heatbed state estimation, executes calibration of Model B, and performs what‑if co‑simulations. The self‑adaptation loop triggers new experiments, re‑configures the controller, and updates the twin. Resource management is handled by the Pi’s CPU and memory; computationally intensive tasks (Kalman filter, simulations) can be offloaded to a more powerful host if needed. System‑wide coordination is achieved through the RabbitMQ broker and a publish/subscribe pattern, ensuring loose coupling between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through the RabbitMQ broker, which can be deployed on premises or in the cloud. The digital twin publishes and consumes messages via AMQP, enabling interoperability with other components that can subscribe to the same broker. No explicit MES, SCADA, or other enterprise system integration is described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT comprises two analytical thermal models of the incubator. Model A is a two‑parameter model (C_air = 616.56 J K⁻¹, G_box = 0.65 J K⁻¹) that captures the bulk air dynamics. Model B extends this with two additional parameters for the heat‑bed (C_heater = 33.65 J K⁻¹, G_heater = 0.87 J K⁻¹) and is used for high‑fidelity simulation and state estimation. Both models are calibrated against a data trajectory generated by a controlled experiment in which temperature readings from three sensors (T1, T2, T3) are collected every few seconds and transmitted via RabbitMQ to the DT. The DT stores the raw sensor data in a time‑series database, uses calibration algorithms to update the model parameters, and feeds the updated models into monitors and a Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detector. The models are also used in what‑if co‑simulations to optimise controller parameters.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heat‑bed. The low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi interfaces directly with the PT hardware and publishes actuator states to RabbitMQ. The controller implements a bang‑bang logic with four tunable parameters: lower limit (LL), upper limit (UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). Commands are sent over RabbitMQ, allowing remote control of the fan and heat‑bed. The safe protocol embedded in the driver ensures that actuator commands are bounded and that the system is never driven beyond safe temperature limits. Addressing is handled by message routing in RabbitMQ; each actuator state is published to a dedicated topic, and the controller subscribes to the corresponding command topic.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure consists of three temperature sensors. Sensor T1 is positioned outside the box to measure ambient room temperature; sensors T2 and T3 are placed inside the box at the hottest and coldest positions, respectively. The low‑level driver periodically reads all three sensors and uploads the measurements to the RabbitMQ server for consumption by the DT. No explicit sampling interval or accuracy specification is provided in the documents; the only stated behavior is that the driver reads the sensors and transmits the data via the message broker.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured through an on‑demand calibration experiment that generates a temperature trajectory used to fit the free parameters of Models A and B. The calibration results are compared against measured data (Figure 13) to validate the models. Uncertainty is implicitly addressed by the Kalman‑filter‑based anomaly detector, which estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flags deviations. Error detection is performed by monitoring for violations of safety limits and by the Kalman filter’s residual analysis. Quality assurance includes periodic re‑calibration, co‑simulation of what‑if scenarios, and a self‑adaptation loop that schedules new experiments when anomalies are detected.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensors (DS18S20 1‑Wire digital thermometers) are read periodically by the low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi. The driver uploads the temperature values (in °C, 0.5 °C accuracy) to the local RabbitMQ message broker. The data is transmitted over the AMQP protocol as messages published to a topic/queue that the DT components subscribe to. A safe protocol is incorporated to ensure data integrity; no explicit preprocessing is performed beyond the raw sensor readings. The DT consumes these messages in real time to update its state.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller, running on the Raspberry Pi, receives the temperature measurements from RabbitMQ and decides on control actions (heatbed on/off, fan on/off). It publishes command messages to RabbitMQ, which the low‑level driver consumes. Commands are simple control signals (e.g., HEAT_ON, FAN_OFF) encoded in a lightweight format (JSON or binary). The low‑level driver validates the command against a safe protocol, executes the actuator via MOSFET/optocoupler circuitry, and can optionally publish an acknowledgment back to RabbitMQ. No explicit authentication or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT infrastructure is built around a RabbitMQ broker running on the same Raspberry Pi that hosts the controller and low‑level driver. Communication uses the AMQP protocol over the local Wi‑Fi network (edge/on‑premise). The topology is a simple client‑server model: the controller and driver act as AMQP clients publishing/subscribing to queues. Bandwidth requirements are minimal (temperature data and control commands are a few bytes per message). Latency is not quantified but is expected to be low enough for the bang‑bang controller. RabbitMQ provides reliable message delivery and persistence; no explicit redundancy or security (TLS/SSL) is mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The DT is deployed on‑premise at the edge, running on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (1.5 GHz quad‑core, 4 GB RAM). Computational load is light: the controller and driver are simple Python/Node scripts, and RabbitMQ runs locally. Storage is local flash; the DT stores historical data in a time‑series database (not specified but implied). Scalability is limited to the single Pi; no horizontal scaling or cloud deployment is described. Availability is not quantified; the system relies on the Pi’s uptime and the reliability of RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed by a Kalman filter that uses Model B (the four‑parameter thermal model) to estimate the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature from the measured ambient temperature. The DT also runs co‑simulations (what‑if scenarios) that feed historical or real‑time PT data into the simulation to evaluate controller parameter sets. Decision making is carried out by a self‑adaptation loop: when the Kalman filter flags an anomaly, the DT schedules an experiment (e.g., cooling the incubator, ramping heating), reconfigures the controller, collects data, recalibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs what‑if simulations to optimise controller settings, and finally re‑deploys the updated controller. The DT provides real‑time visualisation of the incubator status via a dashboard that consumes data from the RabbitMQ communication server and stores it in a time‑series database. Confidence levels for the recommendations are not explicitly quantified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol that keeps the device in a safe state by periodically reading temperature data and checking for controller commands via RabbitMQ. The DT framework includes monitoring components that evaluate whether the system is performing safely and optimally; anomalies are detected by a Kalman filter that uses Model B to estimate the unmeasured heatbed temperature. The controller implements a bang‑bang strategy with defined lower/upper temperature limits (LL, UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C) to prevent overheating. Calibration algorithms update the physical and environment models, and the self‑adaptation loop schedules experiments, re‑configures the controller, and runs what‑if simulations to maintain safe operation. These mechanisms provide fail‑safe behaviour, real‑time safety monitoring, and adaptive safety‑critical control.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.

The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature

development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).

Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.

more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
